title: getStats() divides by zero when originalBytes guard fires, producing Infinity ratio
description: The overflow guard at line 201 sets originalBytes = 0 when rawProduct is non-finite or exceeds Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER (e.g. zero-dimension input width=0 or height=0 also yields rawProduct=0 which passes the isFinite check). Line 206 then computes ratio = compressedBytes / 0 = Infinity (or NaN when compressedBytes is also 0). The guard's intent was to protect against bad input, but it introduces the very arithmetic problem it claims to prevent. The fix is to return null or set ratio = 0 (or skip the ratio field) when originalBytes is zero.
evidence: Verified at encode-session.ts line 201-206: rawProduct = width * height * bpp, guarded to 0 on overflow, then ratio = compressedBytes / 0 on line 206. Zero-dimension inputs (width=0 or height=0) also yield rawProduct=0 which passes isFinite and the MAX_SAFE_INTEGER check, so the guard does not protect that case either. JSON.stringify(Infinity) produces null, silently corrupting telemetry.

title: finishWithError() rejects headerDeferred even when ImageInfo is already available
description: When decode_budget_exceeded fires, handleMessage sets this.lastInfo = msg.info (line 318) before calling finishWithError(). Inside finishWithError, headerDeferred is rejected rather than resolved — even though the ImageInfo is present in this.lastInfo. Contrast with finish() at line 427-428, which resolves headerDeferred when this.lastInfo is available. A caller awaiting header() during a budget-exceeded decode will receive a BudgetExceeded rejection instead of the ImageInfo that was successfully parsed. The fix is to apply the same logic as finish(): resolve headerDeferred from this.lastInfo if available, and only reject if lastInfo is null.
evidence: Verified at decode-session.ts line 317-327: decode_budget_exceeded sets this.lastInfo = msg.info then calls finishWithError(). finishWithError() at lines 435-447 unconditionally rejects headerDeferred with the error, whereas finish() at lines 427-429 resolves headerDeferred from this.lastInfo when available. The asymmetry is real: a caller awaiting header() loses valid ImageInfo on budget-exceeded.

title: EncodeSessionImpl constructor starts acquireSlot before checking pre-aborted signal, unlike DecodeSessionImpl
description: DecodeSessionImpl (decode-session.ts lines 85-93) checks abortSignal.aborted BEFORE setting up acquirePromise and returns early — no slot is ever requested. EncodeSessionImpl sets up acquirePromise first (lines 117-122) and only then checks aborted (line 135). For an async tiered scheduler with a pre-aborted signal, the acquire promise is already in-flight when abortHandler() fires. Because initAcquire guards on this.terminated the slot is ultimately not acquired, but a cancelSession() call is skipped (this.scheduler is null when abortHandler fires). For a synchronous scheduler, the slot IS acquired and then immediately cancelled. The inconsistency risks a window where metrics or onMessage handlers may be registered on a session that was pre-aborted.
evidence: Verified: DecodeSessionImpl lines 87-93 guard pre-abort before building acquirePromise and return early. EncodeSessionImpl builds acquirePromise (lines 117-122) before checking aborted (line 135). For a synchronous scheduler, initAcquire runs immediately setting this.scheduler, so abortHandler's cancelSession fires correctly. For an async scheduler, this.scheduler is null when abortHandler fires synchronously (line 136), so cancelSession is skipped — the slot eventually self-cancels via initAcquire's terminated guard, but is a real ordering asymmetry.

category: broken sort/compare — bpp fallback covers unexpected format
title: bpp chain's else-branch silently assigns 16 to any unrecognised PixelFormat
description: The chain `format === 'rgba8' ? 4 : format === 'rgba16' ? 8 : format === 'rgb8' ? 3 : 16` uses 16 as a catch-all for anything that is not the three named formats. Today the only remaining value is 'rgbaf32' (4ch × 4 bytes = 16), so the result happens to be correct. If PixelFormat is ever extended with a new variant (e.g. 'rgb16' = 6 bpp), the else-branch silently miscalculates originalBytes without any TypeScript exhaustiveness error. A switch with a default that throws, or an explicit check for 'rgbaf32', would make future regressions a compile-time or runtime error.
code_excerpt: const bpp = this.opts.format === "rgba8" ? 4 : this.opts.format === "rgba16" ? 8 : this.opts.format === "rgb8" ? 3 : 16;
evidence: Verified at encode-session.ts line 197: the ternary chain's final else arm is 16 which happens to be correct for 'rgbaf32' (4ch × float32 = 16 bytes/pixel). No TypeScript exhaustiveness check exists. Adding a new PixelFormat without updating this line would silently produce wrong originalBytes; however the current state is not a bug, making this a low-severity latent risk.

title: No test exercises getStats() return values, especially the division-by-zero edge case
description: The encode-session test suite never calls getStats() on a completed session. The division-by-zero bug in the ratio computation (logic-1) is therefore undetected by automated tests. A test should emit encode_done with a known totalBytes, call getStats(), and assert originalBytes, compressedBytes, and ratio are all finite numbers. An additional test should cover width=0 or height=0 to verify the overflow guard does not produce Infinity.
code_excerpt:
// No test calls session.getStats() — the entire path from encode_done
// through totalBytesWritten → getStats() → ratio computation is untested.
evidence: Grep confirms no call to getStats() in encode-session.test.ts. The test suite covers lifecycle, cancel, error normalization, and quality/distance defaulting, but the entire getStats() path is untested. Combined with the confirmed logic-1 bug, this is a genuine gap.

title: STAGE_RANK record is not exhaustiveness-checked at compile time against DecodeStage
description: STAGE_RANK is typed as Record<DecodeStage, number>, which would catch missing keys if DecodeStage is extended. However, the stageRank() helper uses a non-null bracket access (STAGE_RANK[s]) with no runtime guard; if a worker ever emits an unrecognised stage string that bypasses the union type, stageRank returns undefined, causing firstFrame's comparison to behave incorrectly. Adding a runtime assertion (or typing the return as number | undefined and guarding the caller) would make the invariant explicit.
evidence: STAGE_RANK is typed as Record<DecodeStage, number> covering all four members of the union. The TypeScript compiler will error if DecodeStage gains a new member that is not in STAGE_RANK. The runtime risk only applies if an untyped wire string bypasses the union, but handleMessage already enforces the discriminated union through the switch on msg.type. The runtime exposure is hypothetical and the compile-time guard is sufficient given the codebase's TypeScript strictness.

title: validateWasmUrl allows javascript: and data: URIs via the blob: prefix check
description: ALLOWED_WASM_URL_PREFIXES checks only that the URL starts with 'https://', 'http://', 'blob:', or '/'. A value like 'blob:javascript:...' passes the 'blob:' prefix check and a value of '/ ' (space then anything) passes the '/' check, but more critically a 'data:' URI is only blocked if it doesn't start with those four prefixes. The list does not block 'javascript:' or 'data:' URIs at all — any string that doesn't start with those four strings is rejected, but those prefixes don't cover every dangerous scheme. Most importantly the '/' prefix allows paths like '/../../sensitive' which in some fetch contexts can trigger path-traversal in server-side rendering environments. The validation is good but not exhaustive for all runtimes where wasmUrl is later passed to fetch() or passed to Worker() constructor.
evidence: 'blob:javascript:...' is not a valid blob URL and browsers/Node reject Worker construction with such a URL. The path-traversal concern via '/' prefix is a server-side rendering scenario outside this library's threat model (wasmUrl is a caller-controlled configuration value, not user-supplied input). The validation is a defense-in-depth guard against obvious mistakes, not a security boundary; the description overstates the risk.

title: validateCapabilities casts value to Record<string,unknown> without guarding against prototype-pollution via __proto__ or constructor keys
description: validateCapabilities receives an 'unknown' value from a worker postMessage (via mod.getCapabilities()). It casts to Record<string,unknown> and then reads named properties via bracket notation (v['wasm'], v['selectedWasmBuild'], etc.). If the incoming object contains a key '__proto__' or 'constructor' with a boolean value, the bracket-access passes the type checks but the object's prototype chain may have been tampered with by a malicious service worker or supply-chain compromise of @casabio/jxl-capabilities. While structuredClone in postMessage generally strips prototype pollution, the value arrives here after an async import of a potentially tampered module, not via postMessage. The cast combined with bracket access on raw untrusted shape is risky in Node environments where the module resolution can be intercepted.
evidence: The value comes from a dynamic import of a same-origin first-party module (@casabio/jxl-capabilities), not from postMessage or external user input. Prototype pollution via bracket access on a capabilities object returned from a same-origin module is not a realistic attack vector. The validation function only reads string/boolean fields for type checking and then casts the original reference — it does not write to any global prototype. Self-reported confidence was 'low', and the threat model assumption (tampered module resolution) is outside this library's scope.

title: computeWorkerCostForWasmUrl reads jxlWorkerTier query-param from caller-supplied URL without sanitizing the param value length
description: parseRequestedWorkerTier and computeWorkerCostForWasmUrl parse the 'jxlWorkerTier' query parameter from the caller-supplied wasmUrl. The tier string is compared against a whitelist of five literals, so the value itself cannot cause injection. However the URL object construction uses 'https://dummy.invalid' as a base for relative URLs, and a very long wasmUrl (e.g. 100 KB of garbage) is fed into new URL() without any length guard. In a Node.js environment with a compromised dependency injecting a crafted ContextOptions, this could be used to cause excessive memory allocation. This is low-exploitability but the URL length is unbounded before parsing.
evidence: wasmUrl is a developer-supplied configuration value (ContextOptions), not user-supplied runtime input. The threat model of a 'compromised dependency injecting crafted ContextOptions' is a supply-chain attack that can do far worse things than allocate a large URL string. Self-reported confidence was 'low'. The URL constructor handles all length inputs safely with no known DoS vector in V8 or Node.

title: newSessionId() falls back to Math.random() when crypto.randomUUID is unavailable, making session IDs predictable
description: The fallback path uses Date.now().toString(36) + Math.random().toString(36).slice(2,10). Math.random() is not cryptographically secure; in environments where crypto.randomUUID is unavailable, an attacker who can observe timing or seed state could predict session IDs. Session IDs are used to route worker messages back to the correct session via the scheduler's onMessage dispatch (sessionId guard in handleMessage). A predicted session ID could allow a malicious worker (supply-chain attack) to inject messages into a different session's handler. The primary defense is that workers are same-origin and messages are not user-supplied, but the non-CSPRNG fallback is still a latent risk in older runtimes.
evidence: Verified at util.ts lines 39-48: the fallback is real and Math.random() is not a CSPRNG. crypto.randomUUID is available in all modern runtimes (browsers, Node >= 19) so the fallback only fires in very old environments. The security risk is low given same-origin workers, but the fix (use crypto.getRandomValues() as intermediate fallback) is trivial and worth applying.

title: handleMessage sessionId guard uses loose string equality against worker-supplied data without verifying the message shape is a valid WorkerToMainMessage
description: The sessionId guard at line 272 casts msg to {sessionId?: string} and compares sessionId against this.id. However the type of msg is WorkerToMainMessage which is a discriminated union; if a malicious or buggy worker sends an object whose sessionId field passes the check but whose remaining fields don't match any expected case, the switch falls through to the default case which only logs in dev mode. The risk is that a crafted message with a matching sessionId but an unexpected type could silently no-op rather than being rejected with a typed error. This is a defense-in-depth gap: there is no structural validation that the incoming postMessage data matches WorkerToMainMessage before it is processed.
evidence: Messages arrive via same-origin workers through the scheduler's onMessage dispatch — they are not externally injectable. The TypeScript type WorkerToMainMessage is enforced at the worker send site. An unknown message type falls to the default case (silent no-op in prod) which is the correct defensive behavior for forward-compatibility (new protocol messages from newer workers should not crash older sessions). Self-reported confidence was 'low'.

title: Worker-supplied error message is truncated to 512 chars but the originalCode wire value is embedded without truncation before the final slice
description: At line 359, originalCode (a raw string from the worker wire protocol) is interpolated into safeMessage before the 512-char slice: `[wire code: ${originalCode}] ${safeMessage}`.slice(0, 512). The originalCode itself is never bounded before interpolation. If a malicious worker sends a very long code string (e.g. 100 KB), the string concatenation creates a large intermediate string in memory before slice() discards most of it. This is a memory amplification issue rather than a true injection, but it could be used to transiently spike memory per session. The correct fix is to also cap originalCode before interpolation.
evidence: Verified at decode-session.ts lines 351-359: safeMessage is capped to 512 chars, but originalCode is interpolated without a prior slice before the template literal is built. A worker sending a 100 KB error code string would cause a transient large string allocation. The fix is one line: originalCode.slice(0, 64) before interpolation. The workers are same-origin so exploitability is low, but the fix is trivial.

title: DecodeOptions fields (downsample, budgetMs, targetWidth, targetHeight) are passed to the worker without numeric bounds validation
description: DecodeOptions fields such as downsample, budgetMs, targetWidth, and targetHeight are forwarded directly into the MsgDecodeStart without any bounds checking (e.g. downsample <= 0, budgetMs < 0, targetWidth/Height values that overflow i32 in WASM). TypeScript types constrain the compile-time surface but callers using plain JS or asserting 'as DecodeOptions' can pass out-of-range values. If the WASM layer does not validate these, it could cause integer overflow or undefined behavior in the C++ libjxl decoder. Adding a lightweight validation step (min/max checks) at the session layer before forwarding to the worker would prevent malformed messages from reaching WASM.
code_excerpt:
const startMsg: MsgDecodeStart = {
      type: "decode_start",
      sessionId: this.id,
      format: opts.format,
      region: opts.region ?? null,
      downsample: opts.downsample ?? 1,
      ...
      budgetMs: opts.budgetMs ?? null,
      targetWidth: opts.targetWidth ?? null,
      targetHeight: opts.targetHeight ?? null,
      fitMode: opts.fitMode ?? null,
    };
evidence: The session layer does not validate numeric bounds on these fields (confirmed in decode-session.ts lines 60-76). Whether libjxl's C++ layer validates them before use is not determinable from the session code alone — it requires inspection of the WASM bridge and decode-handler. The opportunity is real if WASM does not guard these values, uncertain if it does.

title: EncodeOptions width/height/distance/quality/effort are not validated for out-of-range values before being sent to the worker
description: EncodeOptions.width and height can be any number including 0, negative, or values that overflow WASM i32 (> 2^31-1). distance and quality can be negative or NaN. effort can be outside libjxl's accepted range (1-10). These are passed to the worker in MsgEncodeStart without any session-layer guard. getStats() at line 201 already guards against width*height*bpp overflow but only for the stats computation, not for what is sent to libjxl. A session-layer validator would prevent malformed encode requests from causing undefined behavior in the WASM codec.
code_excerpt:
const startMsg: MsgEncodeStart = {
      type: "encode_start",
      sessionId: this.id,
      format: opts.format,
      width: opts.width,
      height: opts.height,
      hasAlpha: opts.hasAlpha,
      ...
      distance,
      quality,
      effort: opts.effort ?? 4,
    };
evidence: Confirmed that encode-session.ts does not validate width/height/distance/quality/effort bounds (lines 65-96). Whether libjxl validates these in the WASM encode path is not determinable from this layer alone. The opportunity is real if libjxl exhibits UB on out-of-range inputs, uncertain without reviewing the encode-handler and bridge.

title: getStats() returns ratio=Infinity when originalBytes is forced to 0 by overflow guard
description: When width*height*bpp overflows Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER (e.g. 50000×50000 rgba16), the guard sets originalBytes=0 and then computes ratio = compressedBytes / 0 = Infinity. The EncodeStats contract documents ratio as 'compressedBytes / originalBytes', implying a finite number. Callers comparing ratio < 1.0 as a compression check will always be false, and logging/serialization (JSON.stringify) turns Infinity into null. The originalBytes=0 sentinel should also set compressedBytes=0 and ratio=0, or the function should return null.
evidence: Duplicate of logic-1 from a contracts perspective. Verified the same code at encode-session.ts lines 195-210. The EncodeStats type does not declare ratio as potentially Infinity, so returning Infinity is a contract violation. JSON.stringify silently converts it to null.

title: DecodeSessionImpl exposes header() and info getter that are absent from the DecodeSession interface
description: DecodeSessionImpl.header() (line 192) and the info getter (line 188) are public methods callable on the concrete class but are not declared on the DecodeSession interface in jxl-core/src/types.ts. Code that holds a DecodeSession reference (returned by JxlContext.decode()) cannot call header() or read info without a cast. Any caller relying on these methods via the interface type will get a TypeScript compile error, and the methods may be silently dropped when the implementation is replaced. The DecodeSession interface should be extended, or these should be explicitly typed as implementation-only and not exported.
evidence: Verified at decode-session.ts lines 188-193: both header() and the info getter are public on the concrete class. The index.ts exports DecodeSessionImpl for test access, but JxlContext.decode() returns DecodeSession (the interface). Callers with only a DecodeSession reference cannot use these without casting. The interface gap is real.

title: Large set of EncodeOptions fields are silently dropped and never forwarded to MsgEncodeStart
description: EncodeOptions in jxl-core/src/types.ts declares many fields (jpegReconstruction, alreadyDownsampled, upsamplingMode, ecResampling, frameIndexing, allowExpertOptions, buffering, modular, brotliEffort, decodingSpeed, photonNoiseIso, advancedControls) that are documented with WASM-bridge instructions. None of these fields appear in MsgEncodeStart (the protocol message) and none are forwarded in encode-session.ts. A caller who sets opts.jpegReconstruction or opts.buffering will receive no error and no effect — the feature silently does nothing. This is a contract promise gap: the public type advertises options that the session layer simply ignores.
evidence: The encode-session.ts code is verified — only the fields listed are forwarded in lines 65-96. Whether the missing fields (jpegReconstruction, buffering, modular, etc.) actually exist in EncodeOptions in jxl-core/src/types.ts cannot be confirmed without reading that file. If those fields are declared in the public type, this is a real gap; if they are in a comment/TODO or not yet in the type, it may be aspirational. Marking uncertain pending types.ts inspection.

title: pushPixels() after cancel/error throws the generic ConfigError, not the real terminal error
description: DecodeSessionImpl.push() was updated (DS-7) to surface the real terminal error from terminalError when the session is terminated. EncodeSessionImpl.pushPixels() (line 150-152) still throws a fixed ConfigError string regardless of why the session terminated — a cancelled session, an encode_error, or an OOM all produce the same 'ConfigError: pushPixels() after finish/cancel/error'. The two sibling implementations diverge on this contract point; encode callers cannot distinguish 'push after cancel' from 'push after OOM'.
evidence: Verified: decode-session.ts line 144 throws `this.terminalError ?? new JxlError(CONFIG_ERROR, ...)` exposing the real terminal error. encode-session.ts line 151 throws a fixed ConfigError unconditionally. The EncodeSessionImpl has no terminalError field at all (only EncodeSessionImpl's terminate() method exists but stores nothing equivalent to terminalError). The asymmetry is real and the fix requires adding a terminalError field to EncodeSessionImpl.

title: decode_budget_exceeded frame is passed to makeFrame() but MsgDecodeBudgetExceeded does not extend DecodeFrameMeta
description: makeFrame() is typed to accept a base shape '& DecodeFrameMeta'. MsgDecodeBudgetExceeded carries only a region field (no sourceScale, progressiveSequence, passOrdinal, etc.) and does NOT extend DecodeFrameMeta in the protocol definition. TypeScript accepts the call because the DecodeFrameMeta fields are all optional, so the type is structurally satisfied. However the contract between protocol and session handler is implicit — if a future DecodeFrameMeta field becomes required, or if the worker starts emitting extra metadata on budget_exceeded, there is no compile-time guard that the message shape was updated. This is a latent drift risk between protocol.ts and the handler.
evidence: The decode-session.ts code is verified — makeFrame() is called with msg (MsgDecodeBudgetExceeded) at line 319. Whether this is a real type mismatch depends on how MsgDecodeBudgetExceeded is declared in the protocol types. If its optional DecodeFrameMeta fields are present in the type (even as optional), TypeScript accepts it structurally. Cannot confirm without reading protocol.ts.

title: EncodeSessionImpl abort signal is checked after acquire rather than before, unlike DecodeSessionImpl
description: DecodeSessionImpl checks opts.signal.aborted BEFORE any scheduler interaction (lines 87-93 of decode-session.ts) and never acquires a slot for a pre-aborted signal. EncodeSessionImpl (lines 125-142) sets up the abort handler and then checks aborted, but by that point the abortHandler calls this.scheduler?.cancelSession() — which requires this.scheduler to already be set. For a pre-aborted signal, scheduler is still null at the time abortHandler() is called synchronously (line 136), so cancelSession is a no-op and the acquire promise is already in-flight. The slot may be acquired and never released if the abortHandler fires before initAcquire sets this.scheduler.
evidence: Verified as the same issue as logic-3 and concurrency-1 from a contracts perspective. For a pre-aborted signal on an async scheduler, this.scheduler is null when abortHandler fires synchronously (line 136), so cancelSession is skipped. initAcquire at line 105 guards 'if (this.terminated) return Promise.resolve()' so the slot is never acquired for the async path — but for a synchronous scheduler, initAcquire runs inline before the abort check and the slot is acquired then immediately cancelled via terminate(). The contracts issue (asymmetry with DecodeSessionImpl) is confirmed.

title: firstFrame() utility is exported from decode-session.ts but not re-exported from the package index
description: firstFrame() is a public utility function exported from packages/jxl-session/src/decode-session.ts (and appears in dist/decode-session.d.ts), but it is not re-exported from src/index.ts or src/browser.ts. External callers who import from '@casabio/jxl-session' cannot access firstFrame() without reaching into the internal module path. If any downstream code does import { firstFrame } from '@casabio/jxl-session/src/decode-session', it bypasses the public surface and breaks on any internal refactor.
evidence: Verified: firstFrame is exported from decode-session.ts (line 478) but neither index.ts nor browser.ts re-exports it. Consumers using the package public entry point cannot access it without importing from the internal path. The fix is a one-line re-export addition to index.ts and browser.ts.

category: enums handled by some consumers not others
title: encode_first_byte_ready message is received but its informational contract is silently dropped
description: The handler for encode_first_byte_ready (line 241) is a no-op comment saying 'time_to_first_byte_ms arrives via a metric message'. If the WASM bridge sends timing data on the encode_first_byte_ready message itself (e.g., a timeMs field), that data is discarded. More importantly, the EncodeSession interface and EncodeStats carry no 'timeToFirstByte' observable. Callers who want to know when first byte is ready must rely on the separate metric message, but the event type is defined in the protocol, implying a richer contract. This is a contract underspecification: the protocol defines the event but the session layer documents nothing about when/whether it fires or what callers can observe from it.
evidence: The comment at line 242 explicitly documents that timing data arrives via the metric message, not this event. This is an intentional design decision — the event is handled as a no-op because the data is routed through the metric channel. There is no data loss; the contract is documented in the comment. Whether EncodeSession should expose a timeToFirstByte observable is a feature request, not a contract violation.

title: No contract test verifies that DecodeSessionImpl satisfies the DecodeSession interface at compile time
description: DecodeSession and EncodeSession interfaces live in jxl-core, but there is no assignability check (e.g. 'const _: DecodeSession = new DecodeSessionImpl(...)' or a typed cast) that would fail at compile time if the implementation drifts from the interface. The existing tests exercise behavior, but header() and info getter are accessible on the concrete class without any type error even though they are absent from the interface. A single const assignment in a test or types file would catch future divergence at compile time.
evidence: Verified: no assignability assertion exists in decode-session.test.ts or any other test file. The contracts-2 finding confirms that header() and info are on the concrete class but absent from DecodeSession interface, which would be caught by such a check. Adding a compile-time assignability check is trivial and would prevent future drift.

title: RequestedWorkerTier is a string union with no version guard; new tier values silently fall back to 'auto'
description: parseRequestedWorkerTier() accepts a jxlWorkerTier query parameter from a caller-supplied URL. Unknown tier strings silently return 'auto' (no error, no log). If a newer worker build adds a tier (e.g. 'avx512-mt') and the older session package is in use, the tier is silently ignored and the wrong worker variant is selected. The function has no mechanism to warn callers about unrecognized tiers. A console.warn in development mode for unrecognized-but-non-empty tier strings would catch mismatches at integration time.
evidence: Verified at tier-routing.ts lines 10-22: parseRequestedWorkerTier matches against exactly 5 known tier values and silently returns 'auto' for anything else including non-empty unrecognized strings. A dev-mode console.warn for non-null, non-matching tier strings would catch integration mistakes with no production overhead.

title: onMetric callback exceptions are silently swallowed in production builds
description: The emitFoldedMetrics helper only logs onMetric exceptions when NODE_ENV === 'development'. In production, a throwing onMetric callback causes the exception to disappear with no trace. This means callers that accidentally throw in onMetric (e.g. due to a stale closure over a disposed object) get no signal that something went wrong. The same pattern is repeated in the 'metric' message case handler at line 376.
evidence: Verified at decode-session.ts lines 254-258: catch block only warns in NODE_ENV=development. The design rationale (consumer callbacks must not break the message pump) is correct, but the production silence is a real debuggability gap. A queueMicrotask(() => { throw e }) pattern would rethrow asynchronously without breaking the pump and would surface the error in production error monitors.

title: getStats() ratio is NaN/Infinity when originalBytes is 0 (zero-dimension image)
description: When width or height is 0 (or the product overflows and is guarded to 0), originalBytes is set to 0. The subsequent line computes ratio = compressedBytes / originalBytes which produces Infinity or NaN without any guard. The overflow guard at line 201 only protects the originalBytes variable but does not prevent the division. A caller receiving a NaN ratio could propagate it silently into telemetry or UI display.
evidence: Duplicate of logic-1/contracts-1 from an errors perspective. Same code at lines 197-206 confirmed. Marking confirmed with the same evidence: the division is unguarded and produces Infinity/NaN when originalBytes=0.

title: Pre-aborted AbortSignal path in EncodeSessionImpl does not guard acquirePromise race
description: When a signal is already aborted at construction time, abortHandler() is called synchronously (line 136). This calls terminate() which sets terminated=true. However, initAcquire already checks terminated before acquiring a slot (line 105), but the abortHandler call happens AFTER the acquirePromise chain is set up (line 117-122). The abortHandler fires synchronously before the microtask for initAcquire runs, so terminated=true is set before initAcquire executes — this path appears correct. However, there is a subtlety: unlike DecodeSessionImpl which checks signal.aborted before constructing the acquirePromise chain entirely (line 88), EncodeSessionImpl always constructs the acquirePromise chain and then calls abortHandler, meaning the no-op catch attached to doneDeferred at line 100 is still needed (it is), but the asymmetric ordering between decode and encode session constructors is a latent fragility if the code is refactored.
evidence: Verified: for a synchronous scheduler with a pre-aborted signal, initAcquire runs synchronously within acquirePromise setup (line 119), so this.scheduler is set before abortHandler fires at line 136. For an async scheduler (line 118: schedulerOrPromise.then), the microtask for initAcquire has not yet run when abortHandler fires synchronously, leaving this.scheduler=null and skipping cancelSession. The finding is confirmed as a real asymmetry, though the practical severity is reduced by the terminated guard in initAcquire.

title: probeCapabilities() discards async errors with only a console.warn — no structured error reporting
description: The probeCapabilities async IIFE catches all errors and only emits a console.warn in the catch block. This is declared intentional (keep conservative defaults), but the failure is entirely invisible in environments without a console (e.g. headless workers, SSR). If @casabio/jxl-capabilities throws due to a version mismatch or import failure, the context silently operates with degraded defaults forever with no observable signal to the caller. There is no way for callers to check whether the probe succeeded or failed.
evidence: Verified at context-base.ts lines 138-143: the catch only console.warns. probeSettled is a private field with no public accessor, so callers cannot determine if the probe succeeded. In headless or SSR environments, console.warn may be suppressed. The conservative defaults mean degraded capability detection (wasmSimd=false, etc.) is permanent and silent.

title: firstFrame() throws a bare JxlError with empty context object when stream ends without a qualifying frame
description: If the session's frame stream ends (via end() or fail()) before any frame matching minStage is observed, firstFrame() throws JxlError('Internal', 'stream ended before requested stage', {}). The empty context object {} means sessionId is missing from the error, making it difficult to correlate the error with the originating session in logs or error-tracking systems. When the stream failed with its own error, firstFrame() will propagate that error from the for-await loop naturally, but when the stream ended cleanly without the required stage, the thrown error has no session context.
evidence: Verified at decode-session.ts line 488: JxlError is thrown with an empty context object {}. The session parameter is typed as Pick<DecodeSession, 'frames' | 'cancel'> which does not include .id, so the sessionId cannot be included without access to the concrete id. However, a session.id could be accessed via (session as {id?: string}).id or by extending the Pick type to include id. The missing sessionId is a real debuggability gap.

category: missing error handling on async operation
title: cancel() silently swallows acquirePromise rejection instead of reporting it
description: cancel() uses acquirePromise.catch(() => undefined) to absorb any rejection from the acquire path. If the acquire failed (e.g. scheduler shutdown), the session is already terminated via the .catch handler in the constructor, so the re-check on line 207 (if terminated) will bail. However, the explicit catch-and-ignore in cancel() discards the rejection value entirely, which means if a caller awaits cancel() after an acquire failure, they get no signal about why — they just silently succeed. This mirrors the encode-session cancel path at line 217.
evidence: The acquirePromise.catch(() => undefined) is intentional: if the acquire failed, the session's constructor catch handler already called fail() with the real error, setting terminated=true. The re-check at line 205 then returns early. The cancel() caller gets void (success) which is semantically correct — the session is already in a terminal state and the caller's intent (cancel) is satisfied. Swallowing the rejection here is not a bug; it prevents a double-rejection scenario.

title: EncodeSessionImpl constructor does not validate width/height are positive integers before use
description: The constructor accepts opts.width and opts.height and forwards them directly to startMsg without any validation. A caller passing width=0, height=-1, or a non-integer (e.g. NaN) will produce a malformed encode_start message that travels all the way to the worker before any error is surfaced. The getStats() guard at line 201 only handles post-encode overflow but does not prevent issuing the malformed start. An early guard in the constructor would surface the error at the call site with a clear ConfigError.
code_excerpt:
const startMsg: MsgEncodeStart = {
      type: "encode_start",
      sessionId: this.id,
      format: opts.format,
      width: opts.width,
      height: opts.height,
      hasAlpha: opts.hasAlpha,
evidence: Verified at encode-session.ts lines 65-70: width and height are forwarded without any validation. NaN, 0, or negative values will reach the worker and potentially cause libjxl C++ to receive invalid dimensions. The error will surface as a worker encode_error rather than a clean ConfigError at the session callsite.

title: encode metric callback exceptions are fully silenced with bare empty catch
description: The encode-session metric handler (line 262-268) uses a bare catch with no body at all. Unlike the decode-session which at least logs in dev mode, the encode-session silently eats any exception thrown by opts.onMetric. This inconsistency means encode-session metric errors are harder to diagnose than decode-session ones.
evidence: Verified at encode-session.ts lines 262-267: the catch block has no body at all (only a comment). decode-session.ts lines 377-380 at least logs in NODE_ENV=development. The inconsistency is confirmed — encode metric errors are completely invisible even in development.

title: No structured way to check whether probeCapabilities settled before decode/encode is called
description: The probeSettled flag is set on CapabilityAwareContext but is only accessible internally. Callers have no way to await the capabilities probe settling before issuing a decode/encode, meaning early calls will always use conservative defaults (e.g. wasmSimd=false). Exposing a probeSettled() method or a readyPromise() on JxlContext would allow callers to wait for the probe and get accurate capabilities before dispatching work. This would also allow test code to assert on probe results without timing hacks.
evidence: Verified at context-base.ts lines 120-145: probeSettled is protected with no public accessor or public awaitable. JxlContext interface (line 27-32) has no readyPromise() or similar. Early decode/encode calls will use conservative defaults including wasmSimd=false which may select the scalar WASM build instead of SIMD, reducing throughput for the first batch of operations.

title: Unhandled message type warning only fires in NODE_ENV=development — production message type gaps are invisible
description: The default case of handleMessage only warns in development mode. In production, a new protocol message type (e.g. added in a worker update) dispatched to an older session handler silently does nothing. This makes it impossible to detect protocol version skew in production deployments. A production-safe structured log or a metrics counter for 'unknown_message_type' would surface such gaps.
evidence: Verified at decode-session.ts lines 385-392: the default case only warns in development. In a production deployment where the worker is updated to emit a new message type before the session package is updated, the gap is silent. A queueMicrotask or opts.onMetric-style hook would allow production observability.

category: missing error propagation / resource leak on exception
title: toTransferableBuffer call in push() is not guarded — a detached SharedArrayBuffer throws uncaught
description: push() calls toTransferableBuffer(chunk) at line 157 after all the terminated/closed/drain checks. If chunk is a Uint8Array backed by a detached or SharedArrayBuffer, the .buffer access inside toTransferableBuffer (util.ts line 32) will throw a TypeError synchronously. This exception would propagate out of push() as an untyped Error rather than a JxlError, breaking the typed error contract promised to callers. The same issue exists in encode-session pushPixels() at line 159.
code_excerpt:
const ab = toTransferableBuffer(chunk);
    scheduler.send(this.id, { type: "decode_chunk", sessionId: this.id, chunk: ab }, [ab]);
evidence: Verified at decode-session.ts line 157 and util.ts lines 30-36: toTransferableBuffer accesses chunk.buffer which throws TypeError for a detached ArrayBuffer. The exception propagates as a raw TypeError out of push(), breaking the typed JxlError contract. The same pattern exists in encode-session.ts line 159. Wrapping in try/catch and rethrowing as JxlError('ConfigError') would fix both.

title: probeCapabilities() has no timeout — a hung getCapabilities() import stalls probeSettled forever
description: The async probe imports @casabio/jxl-capabilities and awaits getCapabilities() with no timeout. If the dynamic import or the capability probe hangs (e.g. Service Worker intercept, slow network for a remote module, or a blocked SharedArrayBuffer SAB probe), probeSettled will never become true. Any internal code that waits on probeSettled (if added in the future) would hang indefinitely. Adding a race against a timeout would make the probe robust.
evidence: Verified at context-base.ts lines 129-145: no AbortController or Promise.race timeout exists. If the dynamic import hangs (Service Worker intercept in browser, or SAB probe blocking on SharedArrayBuffer availability check), probeSettled remains false indefinitely. The finally block at line 142 would not fire until the async chain resolves or rejects.

title: EncodeSessionImpl abort handler fires before this.scheduler is assigned, silently skipping cancelSession
description: In DecodeSessionImpl the constructor guards pre-aborted signals BEFORE setting up acquirePromise (lines 87-93 of decode-session.ts), so an already-aborted signal calls fail() immediately and returns. EncodeSessionImpl does NOT mirror this: it builds initAcquire (which sets this.scheduler asynchronously) and then checks abortSignal.aborted (line 135). When the signal is already aborted, abortHandler() is called synchronously while this.scheduler is still null, so this.scheduler?.cancelSession(this.id) is a no-op. The slot is acquired without ever being cancelled, leaking a worker slot until the scheduler times it out.
evidence: Verified as the same root cause as logic-3, contracts-6, errors-3 — all identify the pre-abort ordering asymmetry between Encode and DecodeSessionImpl. For an async scheduler (Promise<Scheduler>), abortHandler fires synchronously at line 136 with this.scheduler=null, cancelSession is skipped, and initAcquire runs later but the terminated guard at line 105 prevents the slot from being acquired. For a synchronous scheduler, initAcquire already ran inline so this.scheduler is set. The worker slot leak claim holds only for the async scheduler path.

title: TieredJxlContextImpl.decode/encode checks shuttingDown then awaits router.pick, allowing sessions to be routed to a shut-down scheduler
description: decode() and encode() check this.shuttingDown synchronously and then call this.router.pick(priority) which is async and may sleep up to visibleGraceMs (default 16 ms). If shutdown() is called during that sleep, both schedulers will be torn down, yet the session returned to the caller will still attempt acquireSlot on the now-dead scheduler. The slot acquire will fail (caught by the acquirePromise catch), but the session is created and handed back to the caller before the error surfaces — callers have no synchronous indication that the context is shutting down.
evidence: Verified at context-base.ts lines 249-254: TieredJxlContextImpl.decode() checks shuttingDown synchronously then passes this.router.pick() (a Promise<Scheduler>) to DecodeSessionImpl. createTieredSchedulerRouter's pick() can sleep up to visibleGraceMs (default 16ms from line 245) before resolving. shutdown() sets shuttingDown and awaits both scheduler shutdowns — if this runs during the sleep, the session's acquirePromise will fail. The caller already has the session object back, so they see a delayed JxlError rather than a synchronous throw.

category: unawaited task / fire-and-forget with observable side effects
title: probeCapabilities async IIFE is not tracked; it can write this.caps after shutdown() completes
description: probeCapabilities() launches a void async IIFE. shutdown() sets this.shuttingDown = true and the probe checks it before writing this.caps. However, the dynamic import('@casabio/jxl-capabilities') may resolve after shutdown() has already awaited scheduler.shutdown() and returned to the caller. If the import is cached the microtask resolves before the check; if it is a real network fetch it may resolve arbitrarily late. In either case, writing this.caps after shutdown is a benign but untracked mutation. More importantly, there is no way for shutdown() to await the probe, so test code that calls shutdown() immediately after createBrowserContext() may see a dangling timer/task holding the process open in Node.
evidence: Verified at context-base.ts lines 129-145: the probe is launched as void and not tracked. The shuttingDown guard at line 135 prevents writing caps after shutdown, but the async task itself may still be pending. In Node.js test environments, an unresolved dynamic import can hold the process open. A Promise reference stored and awaited in shutdown() would fix both the write-after-shutdown and the test teardown issue.

title: firstFrame() fires cancel() without awaiting it, leaving the session active for one tick after the caller receives the frame
description: void session.cancel() is intentional to avoid blocking the return, but cancel() does await acquirePromise before calling cancelSession(). If the caller immediately starts another operation on the same context (e.g. spawning a new decode) within the same microtask, the cancelled session's slot has not yet been released and may temporarily block new slot acquisition if the pool is at capacity. This is a low-severity window but may produce subtle latency spikes under pool pressure.
evidence: The void cancel() is intentional by design: firstFrame() is async and the caller awaits it. The cancel() itself is async and awaiting it inside firstFrame() would add unnecessary latency. The slot release window is one microtask — too short to cause pool pressure in any realistic scenario. The scheduler's slot acquisition is also async, so the cancelled slot would be freed before a new slot acquire completes. This is documented as acceptable behavior.

title: close() sets this.closed before awaiting acquirePromise, so a concurrent push() that passes the pre-await closed check may still proceed
description: close() sets this.closed = true synchronously (line 163) then awaits acquirePromise (line 164). A concurrent push() that is between its two closed checks (line 138 pre-await and line 150 post-await) will observe closed=true at line 150 and return early — which is correct. However, the issue is the reverse: close() does NOT re-check this.terminated after the await (line 165 only checks terminated). If the session was terminated via fail() during the await, close() still proceeds to send decode_close on line 166 to an already-terminated session. The scheduler's send() is fire-and-forget so this is benign at the wire level, but it produces a spurious message on a dead session.
evidence: Reading the actual code at decode-session.ts lines 161-167: line 165 checks 'if (this.terminated) return' before sending decode_close. The finder's description of the bug ('close() does NOT re-check this.terminated after the await') is incorrect — it does re-check. The code is correct: if terminate() ran during the await, the terminated guard at line 165 fires and decode_close is not sent. Self-reported confidence was 'low'.

title: No test covers the race where AbortSignal fires between acquirePromise resolution and the scheduler's onMessage registration in DecodeSessionImpl
description: The constructor registers onMessage BEFORE calling acquireSlot (correct ordering), but when the scheduler promise path is taken (async schedulerOrPromise), the abort handler's this.scheduler?.cancelSession(this.id) relies on this.scheduler being assigned inside initAcquire. If the signal fires in the microtask gap after the async scheduler promise resolves but before initAcquire runs (e.g. a microtask queued concurrently by the abort), this.scheduler will be null and cancelSession will be skipped. A test using a deliberately-deferred scheduler promise and a signal aborted in a microtask between resolution steps would pin this behavior.
evidence: The race described is real: for the async scheduler path, the abort listener fires in a microtask after the scheduler promise resolves but initAcquire hasn't run yet, leaving this.scheduler=null. The terminated guard in initAcquire (line 98) prevents the slot from being acquired, but cancelSession is skipped. No test covers this microtask-ordering race. The test gap is confirmed.

title: AsyncEventStream silently corrupts state under multi-consumer usage; no runtime guard enforces the single-consumer contract
description: The single-consumer contract is documented in comments but not enforced at runtime. If a second iterator is created (second call to [Symbol.asyncIterator]()) while the first is still active, both share the same waiter slot. A second consumer calling next() will overwrite the first's waiter, silently dropping the first consumer's pending resolve/reject callback, causing that for-await loop to hang forever. Adding an assertion or a usage counter that throws on a second concurrent open iterator would surface the violation early in development.
evidence: Verified at event-stream.ts lines 71-125: the single-consumer contract is documented in the file header but not enforced. A second call to [Symbol.asyncIterator]() creates a new iterator sharing the same this.waiter slot. A concurrent second next() on the cold path at line 104 overwrites this.waiter, causing the first consumer's Promise to never resolve. A dev-mode counter check at the start of [Symbol.asyncIterator]() would catch this violation.

title: makeFrame() allocates up to 10 temporary spread objects per frame on the decode_progress/decode_final hot path
description: Each call to makeFrame() evaluates 10 conditional spread expressions of the form `...(msg.x !== undefined ? { x: msg.x } : {})`. Each non-undefined field creates an anonymous single-property object that is immediately spread and discarded. For a decode with N progressive frames this produces up to 10N short-lived objects per frame, each needing GC. The fix is to use explicit `if (msg.x !== undefined) result.x = msg.x;` assignments on a pre-built object, which emits zero temporary allocations.
code_excerpt:
return {
  stage,
  info: msg.info,
  pixels: msg.pixels,
  format: msg.format,
  pixelStride: msg.pixelStride,
  ...(msg.region !== undefined ? { region: msg.region } : {}),
  ...(msg.sourceScale !== undefined ? { sourceScale: msg.sourceScale } : {}),
  ...(msg.progressiveRegion !== undefined ? { progressiveRegion: msg.progressiveRegion } : {}),
  ...(msg.regionFallback !== undefined ? { regionFallback: msg.regionFallback } : {}),
  ...(msg.progressiveSequence !== undefined ? { progressiveSequence: msg.progressiveSequence } : {}),
  ...(msg.passOrdinal !== undefined ? { passOrdinal: msg.passOrdinal } : {}),
  ...(msg.frameIndex !== undefined ? { frameIndex: msg.frameIndex } : {}),
  ...(msg.frameDuration !== undefined ? { frameDuration: msg.frameDuration } : {}),
  ...(msg.frameName !== undefined ? { frameName: msg.frameName } : {}),
  ...(msg.animTicksPerSecond !== undefined ? { animTicksPerSecond: msg.animTicksPerSecond } : {}),
};
evidence: Verified at decode-session.ts lines 222-238: exactly 10 conditional spread expressions in makeFrame(). Each non-undefined field creates a transient single-property object. In V8, conditional spreads with the empty-object {} fallback are not elided — the {} is a real allocation. For progressive JXL decodes emitting many passes, this is measurable GC pressure on the hot path.

title: Warm-path next() allocates a new Promise<IteratorResult<T>> for every buffered item even though the value is synchronously available
description: Line 95 calls `Promise.resolve({ value, done: false })` on the buffered-item warm path. Despite the comment claiming synchronous resolution avoids allocation, `Promise.resolve()` still allocates a Promise object and schedules a microtask tick, adding per-frame overhead for every buffered frame in a high-throughput decode. In V8, returning a resolved thenable via `Promise.resolve` is slightly cheaper than `new Promise(r => r(...))` but still allocates. For a streaming decode emitting many progressive passes this is measurable. A zero-copy alternative is not possible with the standard `AsyncIterator` contract, but a known pattern is to deliver items synchronously via a callback interface and only return a Promise from `next()` on the cold (suspend) path — which is architecturally distinct from `AsyncIterator`. This is a known fundamental cost of the `AsyncIterator` protocol and is noted as an opportunity rather than a strict bug.
evidence: Verified at event-stream.ts line 95: Promise.resolve({ value, done: false }) allocates both a new object literal and a Promise on every buffered-item read. The comment mentions this avoids 'allocation' but Promise.resolve() is not zero-allocation. Labeled as an opportunity with complex fix difficulty (requires architectural change from AsyncIterator to callback), which is appropriate.

title: emitFoldedMetrics() re-defines an inner closure on every invocation and performs a process.env property lookup per metric in the error branch
description: On every `decode_progress` and `decode_final` message, `emitFoldedMetrics()` defines a new `emit` arrow function (closure over `cb`) and then calls it up to five times. The `emit` definition is a new function object per invocation. Additionally, the `process.env?.['NODE_ENV']` lookup inside the catch branch uses a bracket-access string key each time, which inhibits V8 inline caching compared to a module-level constant. Neither allocation is individually significant, but combined they add consistent overhead on every frame. Extracting `emit` to a private method and caching `isDev` at construction time would remove both costs.
evidence: Verified at decode-session.ts lines 248-265: emit is a new closure allocated per emitFoldedMetrics() call. The process.env?.['NODE_ENV'] bracket-access string key is re-evaluated on every catch (error path only, so not hot in practice). The per-call closure allocation is the more meaningful cost. A private method would eliminate it.

title: TieredJxlContextImpl getMetrics adapter copies 3 fields from the full SchedulerMetrics object on every shouldUseMtImmediately call
description: The inline adapter `{ getMetrics: () => { const metrics = this.mtScheduler.getMetrics() as any; return { poolIdle: metrics.poolIdle, poolSize: metrics.poolSize, poolSpawning: metrics.poolSpawning }; } }` allocates a new three-property object on every call to `canUseMtNow()`. `canUseMtNow()` is called once or twice per `pick()`, and `pick()` is called once per `decode()` / `encode()` invocation in tiered mode. The adapter object itself is created once (in the constructor), but the inner closure reconstructs the metrics sub-object on every call. Since `Scheduler.getMetrics()` already returns an object containing these fields, the adapter could simply return the full metrics object cast to `PoolPressureMetrics` (which is a structural subset), eliminating the copy-object allocation.
evidence: Verified at context-base.ts lines 231-239: the adapter reconstructs a 3-field object on every getMetrics() call. This is called once or twice per pick() per decode/encode. The fix (return this.mtScheduler.getMetrics() as PoolPressureMetrics) eliminates the allocation. Low severity but genuinely trivial fix.

title: Integration latency test for time-to-first-pixel is scaffolded but skipped, so the Section 22 <= 500ms p95 contract has no automated enforcement
description: The test at line 33 ('large raw decode produces a DC frame under the Section 22 latency target') is permanently skipped pending a real codec. Until it runs, any regression in decode-session session-acquisition overhead or scheduler queueing latency is invisible to CI. Even before the real codec lands, a synthetic benchmark driven by a fake worker could measure the scheduling + session setup overhead independently of codec time, giving a lower bound on latency regression detection.
evidence: Cannot verify without reading integration.test.ts, but the finding about a permanently-skipped latency test is plausible given the NEEDS_CODEC guard pattern noted in CLAUDE.md. The test infrastructure gap is real if the test is skipped. Marking uncertain as the actual line content was not read.

title: metric message handler checks opts.onMetric on every metric message without caching the callback reference
description: The `case 'metric':` branch at line 374 reads `this.opts.onMetric` (a property access through two dereferences: `this.opts` then `.onMetric`) on every metric message. For high-frequency metric streams this is a minor but repeated cost. The encode-session has the same pattern (line 261-268). Caching the callback as a private field (set in the constructor and checked once) would collapse this to a single field read with a single branch. This also matches the pattern used in `emitFoldedMetrics` where `const cb = this.opts.onMetric` is done first.
evidence: Verified at decode-session.ts lines 373-383: this.opts.onMetric is read via two property dereferences on every metric message. V8 will likely inline-cache this as monomorphic, so the real cost is minimal; however, caching as a private field in the constructor is idiomatic and consistent with the emitFoldedMetrics pattern in the same file.
